title: 05_fetch_ok_check.jsx
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Surface the Server Error, Don't Swallow It

The order status card fetches `/api/orders/:id` and displays the result. It works perfectly when the order exists. When an order ID is invalid or the order has been deleted, the server returns a 404 with an HTML error page in the body — and the component crashes with a `SyntaxError` trying to parse that HTML as JSON, leaving users stuck on a blank loading spinner with nothing in the error state.

The issue is that `fetch` does not throw on HTTP error status codes. A 404, a 500, even a 503 — `fetch` resolves the promise successfully and hands you the `Response` object. Only network-level failures (no connection, DNS failure, CORS block) cause `fetch` itself to reject. The `.catch()` at the end of the chain will never fire for a 404.

Add the HTTP status check that `fetch` omits so that server errors reach the `error` state and show the user a meaningful message instead of a parsing crash.
traceback:
SyntaxError: Unexpected token '<', "<!DOCTYPE "... is not valid JSON
    at JSON.parse (<anonymous>)
    at Response.json (node:internal/deps/undici/undici:5575:23)
    at order-status.jsx:9

Note: The server returned HTTP 404 with Content-Type: text/html. 
res.json() attempted to parse the HTML body as JSON and threw.
The .catch() handler received this SyntaxError, not the intended 404 error.
correctOutput:
PASS order-status.test.jsx > shows error message when order is not found Rendered:

Not Found

options.label: Check res.ok before parsing and throw a descriptive error if the status is not 2xx
options.resultLog:
PASS order-status.test.jsx > shows error message when order is not found Rendered:

Not Found
options.rationale: `res.ok` is `true` when the status is 200–299 and `false` for anything else. Throwing inside `.then()` immediately rejects the chain, bypasses the `.then(data => setOrder(data))` step, and falls into the `.catch()` handler that sets `error`. The user sees a meaningful error message instead of a crash. This is the standard fetch wrapper pattern because `fetch` intentionally does not treat HTTP errors as promise rejections — you must check the status yourself.

options.label: Wrap the whole fetch call in try/catch using async/await to catch any error
options.resultLog:
FAIL order-status.test.jsx > shows error message when order is not found The catch block fires — but with SyntaxError (failed JSON parse), not a 404 message. Rendered:

Unexpected token '<'...

Note: res.ok is still never checked; the SyntaxError from parsing the HTML 404 body is caught and displayed, which is confusing rather than informative.
options.rationale: Async/await with try/catch is a clean refactor of the promise chain, but it does not fix the root issue: `res.ok` is still unchecked, so `res.json()` still tries to parse the HTML error body and still throws a `SyntaxError`. The catch block now fires, but the error message shown to the user is the JSON parser complaint, not a meaningful "order not found" message. The form changed, the bug did not.

options.label: Add a second .catch() specifically for the JSON parsing step to handle bad responses
options.resultLog:
FAIL  order-status.test.jsx > shows error message when order is not found

  Renders error for the JSON SyntaxError — but a subsequent successful fetch for a valid
  order also intermittently shows the error state before clearing it, because the catch
  handler always fires first and .then(data => ...) receives undefined.

  Error shown: "Failed to load order" (but only for parse failures, not all HTTP errors)
options.rationale: Catching the JSON parse error treats a symptom rather than the cause: it handles the `SyntaxError` that `res.json()` throws when the body is HTML, but it does not detect 4xx/5xx responses that happen to return valid JSON (an API that returns `{"error": "not found"}` with a 404 status would silently call `setOrder` with that error object). The chained `.catch().then()` pattern also makes the control flow hard to follow and creates a sequencing issue where the error state flickers before clearing.

hints: The Fetch API considers a request successful if it gets any HTTP response — 200, 404, or 500. Only network-level failures (no connection, CORS, DNS) cause the fetch Promise to reject. How do you distinguish a successful response from an error response after the Promise resolves?

Line 9: `.then(res => res.json())` calls `res.json()` without first inspecting `res.ok` or `res.status`. The `Response` object exposes `res.ok` (true for 2xx, false otherwise) — throw an error there if `ok` is false, and the existing `.catch()` at the bottom of the chain will handle it.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
The Fetch API was designed around a distinction between *network errors* (no response at all: connection refused, DNS failure, CORS block) and *HTTP errors* (a response with a non-2xx status code). Network errors reject the Promise. HTTP errors resolve it successfully — you get a `Response` object with `res.status` set to 404, 500, etc. This was a deliberate design choice: the fetch was technically successful at the network layer. The implication is that `.catch()` at the end of a fetch chain will not fire for a 404 or 500; it will only fire for a JavaScript exception thrown somewhere in the chain.

When the server returns a 404 with an HTML error page, `res.json()` tries to parse that HTML as JSON, fails with `SyntaxError: Unexpected token '<'`, and *that* exception — not a meaningful HTTP error — reaches `.catch()`. The user sees a cryptic parser error instead of "order not found".

### Why the fix is correct
`res.ok` is `true` when the status is 200–299 and `false` for any other status. Throwing inside the first `.then()` immediately rejects the promise chain, skips the data-processing step, and falls into the `.catch()` handler with a meaningful error. `res.statusText` provides the HTTP reason phrase ("Not Found", "Internal Server Error") without having to parse the body. This pattern is the standard fetch wrapper and generalizes to every HTTP error without needing to enumerate status codes.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and the right mental model: `fetch` succeeds at the network layer regardless of HTTP status, so the status check is always your responsibility. The async/await distractor is the one most juniors reach for — it's a cleaner syntax, but without `res.ok` it just catches the SyntaxError instead of the 404, which is still a confusing user experience. The chained `.catch().then()` distractor shows you recognized that treating the parse error is wrong-level, not the right-level. One hardening note: `res.statusText` is empty on HTTP/2, so in production code you'd typically throw `new Error(res.status.toString())` or parse the error body for an API-level message when available. Approved. ✅
